text: [APPLAUSE]

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
I'm Siobhan Roberts.

And I am John Horton
Conway's biographer.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: And
I'm that person mentioned.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
John Horton Conway.
The book is just out.
It was published on July 14.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Bastille Day.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yes, indeed.
Nodal.

There's a few
copies at the back.

And we'll be happy to
sign them afterwards.

And I also should say, feel free
to interrupt us at any point

if you have a
question pertaining
to what we're talking about.
There's mics in the aisles.

But I thought we could start
by doing a brief reading

from the book as is sort of
traditional on these occasions

because I thought
that would give you
a sampling of the book.

And at once give you
a sense of the process

that poor John endured
throughout the course

of my writing the
book and the process

that I endured in
writing about poor John.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Let me just interrupt

to say I'm a bit
testy already today.

So that might very
well-- I'm supposed

to read these yellow lines
on this piece of paper.

You don't know which the yellow
lines are, but never mind.
I do.
I can see them.

I'm also a little bit
upset, I must say-- well,

more than a little bit-- at the
paranoia of Google demanding
that we produce identification.

Had I known that I was
to produce identification

before being let in, I would
not have accepted the invitation
to talk today.
I'm really serious.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: All right.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: OK.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
Thank you for that.

So this is from the chapter on
the Game of Life in the book.

I thought we'd throw
caution to the wind.

So I'll just read a bit of an
intro to give you some context.

"So all hell broke loose
with the Game of Life

when Martin Gardner, the
'Scientific American'

columnist, wrote his October
1970 column about life.
In the column--"

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Capital life.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
Capital life, yes.

"In the column, Conway
issued a challenge-- $50

for the first person
to find the piece

of circuitry in the computer--
the glider gun-- that would
all but prove life universal.

And the pandemonium has
continued ever since.

On a local level, these days
talking about life with Conway

can cause considerable
perturbation.

Without much warning, he reaches
his threshold and turns testy.
It terrifies--"

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: When he
already is, it gets worse.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
"It terrifies him

that his worst nightmare
might come true,

this his life will-- in the
end-- be reduced to life.

When we met for a session in
the alcove at the Princeton math

department one day,
he tried to prepare me

for the hellish consequences,
i.e., the wobbly
that he was about to throw.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: I don't know
what a wobbly is, but anyway.

It says here, "oh god, I
don't know whether to tell you
now or not."

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: "Oh
sure, get it over with."

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: "Well,
it may be upsetting to you.
It's upsetting to me actually.

I lay in bed probably
about a week ago,

in the early hours
of the morning,

and I've decided, I
have to say, that I'm

becoming fairly disenchanted
with your biography.

It's basically because
recently you've

been asking me these
questions about the Life Game.
And you know something?
I hate the Life Game.

I've really realized I hate
the-- what's that word?--
damned Life Game.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: "Well,
that can go in the book."

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
"Yeah, let me finish.

So I'm scared of the
following thing happened.

I'm scared of becoming
another one of these,

John Conway, inventor of the
celebrated Game of Life."

How many people know about
the celebrated Game of Life?
I can't see.
Oh, they all do, don't they?
[LAUGHTER]

"As I told you, every
time I turn to a book--

a new math book-- I look
up a certain sacred name.

And it says, Conway, Game
of Life, pages 34 to 38.
And that's roughly all it says.

And it's quality assassination,
not character assassination.
I did discover it.

And it was part
of my little life
to have discovered it and so on.

And I don't think it
should be totally excised,

but it seems to be all
that I'm known for.

And I wanted you to
somehow set the record

straight by not doing the
same damn thing again.
I mean, god, I was quite upset.

I was so terrified
that I was thinking

of saying, so to speak,
publish and be damned.

I don't want anything
more to do with it."
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: "May I speak?"

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
"Permission to speak?
No, not yet.
You should appreciate something.

This was never a big deal
as far as we were concerned.

We met a bunch of
graduate students
at Cambridge [INAUDIBLE].

This was just a recreation,
a game we played.

Somehow it became a bit more
important later on, or at least

it did in the eyes
of other people.

I never thought of
it as very important.
I still don't, by the way.

I just thought of
it as a bit of fun.
In a way, I felt ashamed of it.

I don't think it counts in
the mathematical community,

at least in the serious
mathematical community.

I don't think any of
my Princeton colleagues
thinks it's of any importance.
I don't know.

In a way, it doesn't
count for me.

So I wasn't going to cooperate
anymore in this character
assassination.

I wasn't going to answer
any more of your questions
about Life.

Well, I don't mind answering
a few to tell you the truth.

Just let me say--
how can I say it?

It was a genuine
part of my life.

And I told you about my
[INAUDIBLE] time and so on,

but I really want you to
minimize it rather than
maximize it.
OK.
Now speak."
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: "Ha.
Well, the less said, the better.

That seemed the best
means of assuagement.

It's a fool's errand attempting
to reason with John when
he's in a state."

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: And I'm
in a state now, by the way.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
So that gives you
just a sampling from the book.

But now maybe to sort
of backtrack a bit

and give you some
broader context--

this process started
initially when

I was writing my first
book about Donald Coxeter.

And everybody told
me I had to talk
to John Conway about Coxeter.
Yes.

And so it took me
awhile to track him down

because I don't think you were
really using email much then

and you weren't reliably
answering the phone.

So I was going about my research
and everything was turning up
geometry.

And then finally I
discovered that Conway
was going to be at math camp.

I think it was in
Maine that year.

And so I sought you
out at math camp.

I don't know if
you remember that.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
I don't remember
any particular incident.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Right.
So this is Donald Coxeter.

My first book was "King
of Infinite Space-- the
Man who Saved Geometry."

Coxeter was a very sort of prim
and proper Edwardian gentleman.
And as I said--

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Have we
got a picture of him here?
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yeah.
He was up on the screen there.

He was known as the
Man who Saved Geometry.

And then so I
sought Conway out--

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: He was
one of my heroes, by the way.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Right.
Yeah.

And I sought you
out at math camp.

And in contrast to Coxeter, this
is what I found essentially.

You blended in so
well with the campers,
looking exquisitely filthy.

And you hadn't
slept in a few days.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: I should
tell you about math camp a bit.

Math camp was one
of two math camps.

It's one for 16-, [? 17-, ?]
18-year-olds good at math,
nerds, and so on.

And it wanders around the
United States and Canada.

And I used to devote one week to
it every year and another week

to MathPath, which is for
13-, 14-, 15-year-olds--

or maybe 12-, 13-,
14-year-olds-- fooling around.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Mm-hm.

And that's what you were
doing when I found you.
For sure.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yeah.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
But it was great,

because I got you
in your milieu.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
It's so great
that I'm not programmed anymore.

I don't have to read the yellow
lines on this piece of paper.
Sorry.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Right.

And so when I was trying to
interview you about Coxeter,

similarly I had an idea of
what I needed to accomplish.

But you did tell me how much you
loved Coxeter and admired him

and how you consider
yourself an honorary student.

But you also told me
rather a lot about yourself
at that time and place.

And so I thought, a-ha,
here is another book,

because you're such a
fabulous storyteller

and you do like to
talk about yourself.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
You mean liar?
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: No, not always.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: You
caught me out in one big lie.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Ah.

Well, I could read that
section from the book.

Or maybe you should tell
the story about Cromwell.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: No, not yet.
No.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Not yet.
Save that for later.

Well, do you want to say a
few things about Coxeter?
You wrote him a letter.
This is the letter.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Oh, yes.

I wrote him a
letter when I was--

I thought it was when
I was in high school,

but it was apparently when I
was at university-- Cambridge

University-- possibly
in my first year.
I don't exactly know.

Coxeter was a wonderful
man, a geometer,

living in Toronto, which is
how Siobhan got to know him.

But he edited Rouse Ball's
"Mathematical Recreations,"

which I read eagerly at school--
at high school, that is to say,

in Liverpool-- and then wrote
to him with various things.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: A
rather long letter.
It's a five-page letter.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Five-page letter.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yes.
Rather verbose.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Illegible to me now.
But, still.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Mm-hm.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Then I
gradually got to know him.

He's one of a number of
people that I got to know

and knew in the end
for about 50 years.

And, you know, then people
who you know for 50 years
tend to die in their late 90s.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
He was 96, yeah.
Mm-hm.

And so I had met
Coxeter when he was 93.

And I knew him for three years
while I was writing the book.

And then the book
came out in 2006.
And then I--

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: That is
her biography of Coxeter.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yes.
Mm-hm.

And then I went to the Institute
for Advanced Study for a stint
as a Director's Visitor.

And I had previously proposed to
John the idea of the biography,
but you said no, never.
Why did you say no initially?

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Well, I was
married to a certain lady who

I thought would be infuriated by
anything I said about anything.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
Well, by then you

were divorced from
that certain lady
and married to another lady.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yes.
But still--

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: It all
factors in together.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: --it
doesn't stop certain ladies
from-- never mind.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Right.
OK.
But then you changed your mind.

So when I arrived
at Princeton, I
called you up just to say hello.

And you said, hey,
about that biography.
So how did you change your mind?

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Well, there
was a rather strange person--

Matthew [? First-- ?]
at Princeton

who was some kind of
administrative assistant.

And I told him some
story-- I forget

which one it was because I have
a number of stories that just

get embellished over
the years until they
become totally false.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
They're all in the book.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY:
Oh, I don't know.

Fortunately a few
of them aren't.

But anyway, and
Matthew said, oh,

somebody should really
write your life story.

And I said, well, there's this
young woman who was going to,
but I said no.

And he said, well,
why did you say no?

And I said, well--
well, I told him

about the certain lady who
would be infuriated by anything
that was said, so to speak.

And he said, oh, well, look,
you can talk to your biographer

and arrange to keep
clear of anything
that might really upset her.

Anyway, after some
considerable talk--

more or less an
afternoon of talk--
he persuaded me to say yes.

Now Siobhan was-- this
conversation I remember-- maybe

falsely-- took place in
January of a certain year.

And Siobhan was going to
come to Princeton in February

of that year and was
phoning every now and then

to make arrangements
or something.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: I might
say it was September,

but, I mean, who's
going to quibble?
[LAUGHTER]
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Probably.

I said, as I remember, so
that means-- it means nothing.

So when she did
turn up, I-- well, I

had decided to look
at her face somehow.

And when I said I
might be prepared

to say yes or
something, I was going

to [INAUDIBLE] to seeing
something about her face.

And I discovered
later I've never
really looked at her face.
But anyway--

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: You were too
distracted defending yourself.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Passed over.
So, yes, I said, in the end.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Right.

And then something
interesting developed, for me,

in the process of talking
to you so many times.

I was at the Institute
on a number of occasions,

numerous visits over the course
of maybe four or five years.

And I would go over
to the department

and interview John on
any number of subjects,

such as FRACTRAN, which was a
precursor to the Game of Life.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: No.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Well, OK,
but just let me continue.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: It wasn't.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: You
can correct that.

But interviewing
you so frequently

and listening to
your stories again

and your fabulous way of
explaining things in such a way

that even I could grasp
the gist of things

or intuit an understanding-- I
realized that it would really

be a disservice if
I were to approach

the book in a traditional
way and translate my subject,

try and set it up
with a third person

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Did you
say it would or wouldn't?

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: It
would be a disservice.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Disservice.
OK.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yeah.

So instead I just
decided I needed

to capture Conway and distill
him and let him really
come through on the page.

And so what I ended
up doing was allowing

you to speak in the biography
in extended quotations.

And I also convinced
my publisher

to give you your own font,
which was no small task,

I may say, because it made
the production and layout
of the book more difficult.

So John has his own font in
the book, comes in and out.

We pass the narrative
baton back and forth.

And I leave it to you
to explain things,

like the surreal numbers
and the free will theorem

and all the various
mathy gems and nerdish

delights that you're
so passionate about.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yeah.

I don't know what I
can say right now.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Well,
maybe you could tell us

a bit about the surreal numbers
or the free will theorem.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yes.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
The surreals are
the thing you're most proud of.

I mean, the Game of Life,
we can write that off.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: I'll
just tell you roughly
what the surreal numbers are.

I suppose, more or
less, everyone here
knows what the real numbers are.

So that's part of
the surreal numbers.

The word surreal
wasn't chosen by me.

It was chosen by
Donald Knuth, who

wrote a little book called
"The Surreal Numbers"--

or called "Surreal
Numbers" actually.

They form an enormous field of
numbers-- absolutely enormous--

that contains the real
field and lies above it.
Hence the term surreal.

But the term surreal also
carries the notion of bizarre
with it.

And they are a
little bit bizarre.
Well, quite a lot bizarre.

They also include Cantor's
infinite ordinal numbers,

which are not very
familiar to people.

So I suppose you know
nothing about them.

Cantor did a wonderful thing
in the decade-- Georg Cantor,

famous German mathematician--
did a wonderful thing
in the decade from 1870 to 1880.

He founded the theory
of transfinite numbers,

or just infinite, if you
like-- past the finites.

Everybody else stopped counting
somewhere at the finite stage.

Cantor actually copied
the usual way of counting,
starting at one.

But it's nicer to
count starting at zero.
So I'll start at zero.

It doesn't make very
much difference.
Zero, one, two, dot, dot, dot.

Everybody stopped,
say, at 2,417,
or some other finite number.

Cantor carried on,
imagining counting

an infinite collection,
getting to the end,

and then you have
a problem of what

the next number is after all
the finite whole numbers.

And Cantor originally
called it infinity,

but it doesn't behave
like the infinity
that people know from calculus.

So after a time he
rubbed a little bit

of the infinity symbol off and
took the Greek little omega

as his notion for the first
infinite number counting
number, omega.

And then there's omega
plus 1, omega plus 2.

You see, you can't
afford to go on inventing
different names all the time.

You know, there are an awful
lot of finite numbers even.

And so there's the
number we-- well,

in England we call
"a hundred and one."

In America, you call
"a hundred one."

Really "a hundred and one"
is a little bit nicer, or 100
plus 1, 100 plus 2, and so on.

We haven't invented separate
names for all of those.

Once we got 100, 101
is the next number.

So with Cantor, omega
plus 1, omega plus 2,

dot, dot, dot, omega
plus n typically,

and then omega plus omega,
which is omega times 2.

Omega times 2 plus 1,
omega [SOUND EFFECT].

It goes on and on and on
and on and on and on and on.

They're wonderful things,
Cantor's ordinal numbers.
It means counting numbers.

And then I found
some other numbers.

In fact, I found more numbers
than anybody else-- far
more numbers than anybody else.

In particular, there's
an infinitesimal number--

1 over omega and so on and
omega plus pi and omega minus 1
and all sorts of other things.
So it gets a bit surreal.

It's so horrendously
large, this world

of numbers, that I went around
in a daydream for about six
weeks-- permanent daydream.

I could see these numbers
spread out in front of me,
or at least I tried to see them.

And if was a huge world--
really huge-- of numbers.

And I must admit that I thought,
hm, you discovered it, John.
Nobody else had ever seen it.
I'm John, by the way.

And there's a thing
about [? stat courses ?]

crossing the American continent
and seeing the Pacific finally.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
Of all he surveyed.

Well, I was [INAUDIBLE]
of all he surveyed too--

of all I surveyed,
perhaps I should say.

I wondered whether Cantor
had had similar feelings.

Cantor eventually-- well, he was
institutionalized several times

and died in a lunatic asylum
in Halle, which later became
part of East Germany in 1918.
A wonderful man.
Yeah.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: And
you said that you're

disappointed that the surreals
haven't found a broader
application so far.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: I am.

You see, Cantor's
infinite numbers

have found applications really
just within mathematics,
but still applications.

It's only in
mathematics that you
want to count infinite sets.

So I feel that the
surreal numbers

are so natural that they will
find an application someday.

The truth is I'm
probably going to be dead
when they find an application.
And I don't really like that.

I was very pleased
to see that you

quote Peter Sarnak in the book
as saying the surreal numbers
will be applied someday.
It's just a question of when.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS:
When and how, yeah.

Now you had an encounter,
shall we say, with Kurt Godel.

You had read
something that he said

that gave you hope that they
might crack open the continuum
hypothesis.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Godel
was the famous mathematical

logician-- in fact, the
mathematical logician
since Aristotle.

Possibly one has to go
all the way back there.

But anyway, he found
a tremendous amount
about mathematical logic.

And in 1940 something-- I
always thought it was 1944,

but I think it's 1947-- he
wrote a paper called "What is
the Continuum Problem?".

The continuum
problem was the first

of the problems--
what were they called?

The Hilbert problems set
forth at the beginning

of the 20th century for
mathematicians to work on.

It was sort of solved
in a sense in that it's

known that it doesn't follow
from the other axioms of set

theory and nor
does its negative.

But that's not really
saying it was solved.
It was unsolved in a way.

But Cantor said in this
paper in the 1940s--
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Godel.
Godel said, yeah.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Sorry.

Godel said that
perhaps we would only

know the solution of
the continuum problem

when we had the correct
theory of infinitesimals.

And I thought I had the correct
theory of infinitesimals.

So when I met Godel-- and
I can't quite remember--
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: In the '70s.
Early 1970s, yeah.
JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yeah.

Well, I was overawed by meeting
the man, to tell you the truth.

But I was keen to say I seem
to have the correct theory
of infinitesimals.

Now tell me how that affects
the continuum problem.

And he said, did
I really say that?
Oh well, I was wrong.
[LAUGHTER]

Well, it's not kind to laugh
so uproariously because it
sort of shattered me in a way.
SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Yeah.
But I think there's still hope.

I mean, there's been
people who've tried.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: I mean,
yeah, I think he was wrong,
too, actually.

I mean, I couldn't see any
effect of it on the thing.
That's why I really asked him.

I mean, I couldn't
see any relation.

And neither could he when he saw
what the surreals were about.

I think he agreed that they
were the correct theory
of infinitesimals.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: Now
the other contribution

that you're very proud
of is your free will
theorem with Simon Kochen.

Do you want to tell
us a bit about that?

And then we can
take some questions.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: That's
a very strange theorem.

It says subject to certain
facts about physics that are not

contentious among
physicists that if we have

free will-- that is to
say human experimenters
have a little bit of free will.

They don't have
to have very much.

That so do elementary
particles have
the same amount of free will.

It's not so terribly
clear what free will is.

But I'm just going to say I
don't believe my action there

in picking up this
whatever it is

and dropping it on the
floor was determined

by the positions of the
particles and momentum

and so on of particles at
the time of the Big Bang.

I think that's really
rather ridiculous.

And that's what strict
determinism says.

And in Newtonian
mechanics that was true.

[INAUDIBLE] the greatest
expert on Newtonian mechanics

said in several different
places that if an Intelligence--

this Intelligence was spelt
with a capital I-- knew

the positions of all
positions on momentum

and so on, of all particles in
the world at any time t zero,

then that Intelligence
could deduce--

had to be pretty
intelligent, by the way--

could deduce their positions and
momenta at all related times.

That's the property of
Newtonian mechanics.

And therefore in
Newtonian mechanics--

if we suppose the Big
Bang known at the time

of Newtonian mechanics--
it would be true

that that action of mine in
dropping the whatever it is--

the Android something
or other-- on the floor

was determined at the
time of the Big Bang.

I think that's absolutely
ridiculous to suppose that.

But anyway, so let me tell
you what the free will
theorem says now.

I believe-- but it's
irrelevant what I believe,

so I shouldn't
even have told you

what I believe-- that
human experiments have

the property that they
compress-- suppose I have
a little box on this table.

It's got a red button
and a green button,

or button A and button
B. Then I believe

that I can press either button
A or button B by a free will

decision that's independent
of the entire past history

of the universe
before I press them.

That I call one bit of free
will-- one binary digit.

I can make a binary choice
independent of the past history
of the universe.

If that is true and various
things about quantum mechanics

and relativity are true-- or
even approximately true-- then

it follows that a spin one
particle that I'm experimenting

on can emerge along
either channel [? C ?]

or channel D in a manner
independent of the past history
of the universe.

That's to say if I have
a certain property,

this spin one particle has
essentially the same property.
It can make a binary decision.

Well, it doesn't actually
make a binary decision.

It doesn't have to think,
what am I going to do?
and then do it.
It just does it.

It does either C or D
in a manner independent

of the past history
of the universe.
That's a theorem.

That's the free will
theorem that I'm saying.

It depends on some
uncontentious facts

about physics, which it's
probably not a good idea for me

to enumerate right now, because,
I mean, as long as you know

that they're there-- and
physicists in general,
have no problem with those.

SIOBHAN ROBERTS: And the

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: It can, yes.

Actually there's a
strong version of it--

the most recent version of it
is called the strong free will
theorem.

When we numerated-- well,
one particular-- one

of these uncontentious
properties

turned out to be
pretty contentious.

That's the property
that I thought
would be the least contentious.

It says, roughly, that
information cannot be

transmitted faster than
a certain finite speed,

which you know will
be the speed of light.

However, that gave rise
to a lot of discussion

among a certain
group of physicists.
What does it mean, information?
Et cetera.

We thought that that would be
the least troublesome thing,
but apparently not.

However, most physicists-- it's
slightly fringe physicists who
find it problematic.

Oh, look, there is-- sorry.

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yes.

In the free will theorem itself,
in the first version of it,

we prove that subject--
well, I stated it

before that if human

free will-- one
bit of free will,

they can make one
binary decision-- then

elementary particles, in

has the same kind of free will.

And that just means
independence of past history.

You can make this decision
independent of past history.
Well, now-- OK.

That implies, by an argument I
call the backgammon argument,
that even-- how can I say it?

Even if the world is
deterministic but based

on random numbers,
then still that
will not simulate the world.

I mean, straight off it says the

The physical experiments

But now people
have always opposed
to determinism randomness.
OK?
But randomness doesn't cut it.

If you suppose-- I mean,
it's easily-- I'm sorry.

I'm finding it's

But even if you suppose that

that God is playing dice,

so to speak, and
those dice affect

what particles are doing, that's
not a sufficient explanation.

The universe cannot be operated
or simulated by deterministic

laws plus random numbers
injected into the universe.

AUDIENCE: Is there
proof of that?

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yes, and I

And you can follow
the proof of it.

Here is the way I believe
backgammon tournaments were
conducted at one time.

What you need to
know about backgammon
is comparatively small.
It is a game played with dice.

And whether a move is legal
or not depends on what
the dice [INAUDIBLE].

And backgammon
tournaments used to be

conducted in the
following way, I believe.

Well, you have, say, 50 tables
at which people-- at each table

two opponents are sitting
ready to move things.

And the tournament director
says, the first throw is,
let us say a three and a five.

And at 50 tables the person who
is first to move at that table

makes a move-- legal--
for a three and a five.

And then the tournament
director says, the second throw
is double six.

And then at the 50
tables the other player

makes a move-- legal--
for double six.

So I hope you understand
the situation.

Now suppose instead
of doing that,

instead of throwing
the dice-- sorry,

perhaps I should
say my model so far

director throws the dice

and says the first throw is a

But instead of doing that,

I'm going to say it obviously

six and so on, and then just

reads them out on the next

place?

That doesn't make
any difference.

I hope people will see that that
doesn't make any difference.
OK.

So suppose God sat
up in his hotel room

the night before the universe
was constructed-- or started--
and threw the dice.

All dice throwing can be

OK?
So do you understand?
I mean, so--

JOHN HORTON CONWAY: Yeah.

And in Cambridge

Let me say I never understood

But I did notice that

Go broke up into a

And I had been
meaning to analyze

the notion of sum of smaller
games for quite some time.

And that prompted me
to actually do it.

And then I discovered,
in particular,

that some games
behaved like numbers.
I now say [? were ?] numbers.

And notice that you've
got a simpler development

of the theory of real numbers
by just regarding them as games.

There's an application
of childish game
theory to numbers.

But it didn't stop
at the real numbers.

It went on to the
surreal numbers.

And that's a lovely thing
for me because I like-- well,

in England we say taking the
[? mickey ?] out of people.

I mean, it means-- I suppose
it means taking the microphone,

It means making fun of
people who are particularly
pompous or something.

And here is this serious
theory of the foundations

of the real numbers
and everything

which was developed in the 19th
century by people with beards.

And it came out-- I mean,
this is a simpler theory

than that that came
out of studying

childish games in a way
that people hadn't thought
was worthwhile before.
So there's an application.

It's not quite what
you're talking about.

In fact, it's an application--
you were talking about sort

of [INAUDIBLE] [? implies ?]
[? C, ?] B applied to C.

This is A applied to
B. But it's amazing.

I find mathematics-- I've
always found mathematics

because I don't
know where it all comes from.

I don't know what the ontology
of mathematical ideas is.
Where is two or three or omega?

Wherever they are, it's a place
that has a certain stability.

I like to compare
numbers to cats.

Well, you see, there's
no doubt that cats exist.

And part of the reason
I'm sure about that

is that when you push
a cat, it pushes back.

It doesn't want to go in
the direction you push it.
It wants to do its own thing.
The same is true of numbers.

I mean, you can't make
numbers do what they don't
want to do in a certain sense.

Of course, numbers are different
to cats in some other senses.
I realize that.
[LAUGHTER]

When you're a
little toddler, you

scream to your
mother about things.
I don't know.
And your mother responds.

Your father might
respond too, but anyway.
You don't scream at the wall.

The wall interferes with

You just can't walk through
walls, but you don't try.

And you don't scream at the wall
in order [INAUDIBLE] let you.

What I'm trying to
say is obstinacy

of things is some kind of

OK.
Well, I don't know.

I've been worried

in which mathematical notions

and [? abilities ?]

and so on of the way in

How do they exist?

And the existence--
People are very confused.

They aren't.

A concept means something

Well, two was there a long time

The statement that Mars has

So abstract things are

The concept of a unicorn exists.

My.
